CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Since last writing we have received a large number of
articles on various towns. It is curious that so far we
" have not received two articles on any one town. Some
of the articles, as might be expected, are not up to pub-
lication mark, but many of them are. This week we
publish an article on Drogheda and some notes on Tulla-
more ; next week we will go across country and publish a
very interesting article on Ennis, the capital of the
Banner County; the following week we will give the
North a look-in with an article on Enniskillen. We had
better not announce further than that, but interesting
articles we have in humd awaiting their turn are on
Athenry, Fermoy and Bantry. We already can see that
our idea has caught on, and that the outcome will be a

. series of interesting and useful articles on scores of towns

in Ireland, that while they provide entertaining read-
ing, may also be safely calculated to do a large amount
of practical good.

“ Sergeant, Cooke is about to withdraw from the
position of sergeant at the Blackrock Police Barracks,
which he has held for eleven years.”” So we are in-
formed by a circular emanating from 67 South Mall,
Cork, and signed by ‘“ faithfully yours,”” James C. Mac-
Namara, F. Hall, James Murphy, D. J. Daly, J. A. F.
Gregg, Clerk; T. B. Lillis, H. L. Tivy, members of
the Committee, and by Charles Jermyn, hon. treasurer,
and J. A. Moore, secretary. The Blackrock over which
his Royal Highness, Cooke, the policeman, presided is
Blackrock near Cork City. This ‘‘ nation-builder
has touched the generous chords in the patriotic hearts
of the gentlemen whose names we have mentioned, and
““Dear . Sir (or Madam)’’ is informed that, It has
been felt thab some recognition should be made of his
(the policeman’s) services, as he has discharged his
duties conscientiously, and to the satisfaction of all
living in the neighbourhood.”” When will the hat be
sent round for a testimonial for the paddling peeler of
Dripsey, we wonder? In London a man might ‘‘ tip ””
a bobby tu’pence, but bobbies are so ‘‘ tony ’’ in this
country that the circular tells us—‘‘ It is desired that
subscriptions should not exceed one guinea, and should
be sent in as early as convenient.”” A brake is placed
on the gushing generosity of the Blackrock subjects of
his retired, or retiring, majesty, Cooke, the peeler.
““ A general meeting of the subscribers will be called at
as early a date as possible to determine. upon the form
which the testimonial should take.”” The begging
letter from which we are quoting is dated 22nd October,
1907. The water-mark on the paper, by the way, is
something like the lion and the unicorn. When will
these public nuisances and impositions in the shape of
begging letters, for well-paid bobbies with pensions
before them, cease? This country is absurdly over-
policed, and if the police were under the people’s con-
trol, they misht be sent killing microbes what time
they now spend in trying to kill trout or eels, they
might be collecting statistics instead of playing concer-
tinas, and catching real criminals instead of polishing
their boots in foreign blacking or creasing their poles
with foreign combs.” The police in this counutry are,
for the most part, a wasteful and ridiculous excess, and
yet Charles Jermyn, 67 South Mall, Cork; J. A. Moore,
James C. MacNamara, F. Hall, James Murphy, D J.
Daly, J. A. F. Gregg, Clerk; T. B. Lillis, and H. L.
Tivy, whoever these persons may be, sent out a begging
letter—in many cases we suppose it will, for practical
purposes, be a semi-intimidating letter—to unfortunate
tax-payers already groaning under the exorbitant ex-
pense of keeping up a plethora of policemen. The
intended victims are told that the subscriptions ‘‘ should
not exceed one guinea.”” And all this is for a bobby
who has ““reigned”’ in Blackrock, Cork, for eleven
years!

In the course of a personal letter a correspondent
writes :—‘‘ For example, my son will require a very
costly outfit suited for a five years’ sojourn in—and
we are insisting (according to your directions) that
they are, as far as may be, of Irish manufacture—Drip-
sey rugs, Irish tweed for suitings, Irish linen shirts,
poplin  ties, Irish-made boots—everything, in fack,
advertised by the LEADER, and duly attributed to it, too.
So with every.good wish that all your readers may
follow my example when occasion offers.’”

* We don’t think that Mr. Edward Martyn’s party can

“be congratulated on their performance at Dublin on

Sunday. ' So far as we can judge by the x?e\vspa?ell;
“reports, the Arran Quay Branch of the United Illlgsh
League called a public meeting at Smithfield, at whic!
the Lord Mayor, Mr. William Field, M.P. ; Mr. Devlin,
M.P.; Mr. T. Harrington, M.P., and Mr. Sherlock,
T.C., spoke. Apparently as soon as this meeting com-
menced some of Mr. Edward Martyn’s followers—Alder-
‘man Kelly, Mr. P. T. Daly, T.C.; Mr. O’Carroll, T.C.,
and Mr. J. T. O’Kelly, T.C.—drove up in a_brake to
hold another opposition meeting. ~The police came
between the U.I.L. people and Mr. Martyn’s followers,
and, no doubt, prevented a street brawl. As it was,
we read that—‘‘ For a length of time both sections V]e(l
with each other in a booing, cheering, and groaning
competition, etc.””  Again, < Matters certainly looked
very hot when a big, fresh arrival of D.M.P. men had
the effect of keeping any of the warmer spirits from
breaking out into a collision. Nothing worse than a
deafening tumult and a succession of regrettable scenes,
however, occurred.”’ In this case Mr. Edward Martyn’s
followers appear to have gone specially to kick up a
tumult. They would be quite entitled to call a meeting
of their own in some other part of Dublin; but we
wonder that an educated man like Mr. Edward Martyn
allows his followers to go, many of them armed with
hurley sticks, and hold an opposition meeting close by
the meeting organised by the U.I.L. All this sort of
thing helps to disgust the public, but so far as sympathy
goes in this particular instance, it will go with the
United Irish League, for Mr. Edward Martyn’s fol-
lowers were the aggressors. Why did not Mr. Edward
Martyn and Mr. John Sweetmau head their followers
on the occasion? These people say they want to con-
vert all Trishmen to their ‘‘ policy,”” whatever that may
be ; certainly they are going a curious way about it.

It is significant that though Mr. Edward Martyn’s
followers are ready to act as they did on Sunday at
Smithfield, and give work to the police in keeping order
between fellow-Irishmen, that they made no attempt to
break up the late unlamented ‘‘ International ’’ Exhibi-
tion ; we believe they only passed resolutions against that
at a safe distance. Let us say that we would not think
any the better of them if they did attempt to break up
the Exhibition, as those who were able to carry through
the Exhibition were entitled to do so without rowdy
obstruction ; but it is significant that whilst Mr. Mar-
tyn’s followers made no demonstration against the Exhi-
bition, some of them sallied forth in a waggonette to
Smithfield on Sunday when fellow-Irishmen and
Nationalists were holding a public meeting. Of course,
the police attendcd in force, and protected them; but
the whole incident makes humiliating reading for
theughtful and patriotic Irishmen.

Referring back for a momen! to the articles which we
wrote on Kilskeery parish, in Tyrone, we neglected to
point out that the parish can boast of a fine flute band
as well as a string band. The flute band is quite an
old institution, and as the members are good Gaels, we
would not liz2 to pass them over without notice. Mr. S.
McGill,, the County Tyrone Instructor in Horticulture,
has given great help in the school garden schemes in
Kilskeery, as he did previously in Dromore; and Mr.
Dallinger, Secretary of the Tyrone County Council, has
also placed Father Maguire’s parish under a debt of
gratitude:

We wonder how many, if any, Irish manufacturers
are a penny the better for the helter-skelter, water-
chute, etc., that closed up on Saturday last. Probably
_a great many of them who went into it did not make

their expenses. However, it is all over now, and the

time for the call on the unfortunate guarantors is about,

to come. We hear, though we cannot say whether the
report is true or not, that the call on the guarantorsis
likely to be one of £75,000.

When the site is being laid out for a'public park
perhaps it could be arranged that a'[e\v restaurants with
open-air accommodation on the continental models might,
be erected on the grounds. The site is an admirable
one, and the new park would o[fel: a fine opportunity
for introducing the open-air café into Dublin.  The
example might be followed. ~We don’t suggest that
there should be any licenses granted tq Testaurants in
the park, but it would be a goqd thing probably if
Dublin got into the habit of taking its drinks in the
open air when feasible ; it would probably make for tem-
perance. A man who sits out in the open, and orders
his drink, is a different class of person to the one who
likes to frequent a place that has an entrance up some
lane-way through which he can dart now and again
without the probability of being seen by the public.

“ A Reader of the Leaper’’ who wrote us from
Nenagh did not give his name and addvess.

A correspondent writes:—‘‘ You will be pleased to
hear that, as a result of the LEADER representations,
supplemented by questions in the House of Commons, a
contract has now been placed with a Belfast firm for the
creosoting of imported telegraph poles, which was for-
merly done in Great Britain. Did a Dublin firm decline
to tender for the work, or did it receive an invitation to
tender as promised by the Postmaster-General? But why
should poles be imported from the Continent? Is none
of the native timber suitable? I would suggest to the
Industrial Development Association that they press the
Postmaster-General to give a trial to native-grown
timber, which would greatly encourage forestry indusiry
in Ireland. Tt is also to be hoped that the Post Office
will continue to be pressed to extend the policy of local
contracts for Irish requirements to such articles as
bicycles, cloth, boots, tools, etc. Would it not be worth
while for one of our pottery manufacturers to try and
produce insulators suitable for electric lines, stoneware
and porous jars for batteries, etc.?”’

In a few weeks’ time our Christmas Number will be
issued, probably in the second week in December, but
we have not finally decided yet. We are going to make
a new departure this year. In previous years we
charged 2d. for the Christmas Numbers, and our readers
can bear testimony that all of them were utterly cheap
at the price. This year we want to make a splash. We
won’t bother about a special cover or a sevies of cartoons,
but we will put the Double Number out and sell it at
1d. as a Christmas box to the readers. Forty-eight
pages, containing of course a limited number of adver-
tisements, for 1d.! There’s Irish enterprise for you!
We want to induce a large number of people who never
read the LEADER to buy our Christmas Number. The
Leaper stands for Irish Ireland, and Trish Ireland has
a lot of territory to conquer yet, and we don’t see why
thousands of people who are so far outside the influence
of Trish Treland and have not read the Leaper should
not be induced to read it, and by doing so become Irish
Irelanders. The LEADER is the organ of Irish Ireland.
Parties are split up into fragments these days, and, un-
fortunately, we fear that a large number of people are
becoming disgusted with all the parties, and are pre-
disposed towards the microbe of apathy. The Irish

Parliamentary Party, William O’Brien’s Party, the De-
volutionists, the ‘‘Hungarian,”” or as they are now called
1n neither good English nor good Irish, ‘* Sinn Feiners,””
: haye all their pros and their cons, but Irish Ireland, for
‘ which the LEADER stands, towers independent and above
them a.ll:_ It embraces the industrial revival,.the lan-
guage revival, the thought revival, the backbone revival,
anq the fight for tolerance. Now as we have decided
to issue our Christmas Number for a penny, we hope,
at least, to be recompensed somewhat by the paper
getting into thousands of now hands. Our thousands of
staunch and enthusiastic friends. might blow the
trumpet when they have an opportunity, and by doing
150 be the immediate means of snatching a brand from
the regions of West Britain. Of course, though at
heart we are what it would not become us to say, yet
we have to pay our printers and other bills, and if we
give a Christmas Double Number for a penny, we would
like the satisfaction of knowing that the Lzaper had
penetrated in fresh thousands into even Darkest West
Britain. Our regular readers could help us, and we
feel sure they will. We guarantee that we will give
wonderful value for the penny. Agents should order
early, and ordinary readers who may want extra copies
to send to their friends at home and abroad, should in-
sure that they are not disappointed. We might gently
hint to intending advertisers also that they would do
well to secure space as early as possible.

What a pity proper University facilities and native
government do not open up adequate careers in Ireland
for the young men of brains in this country. We see
that a young ‘‘ Idolator >’ from Belfast has passed for
““Student Interpretership in the Far East.” This is
the first time for thirty years that a Belfast man has
secured this distinction. Mr. Gerald Byrne not only
passed, but took first place in Ireland and second place
in the Empire, from all parts of which, we understand,
there were competitors. It is, in a sense, gratifying to

_see a young ‘‘ Idolator,”’ despite all disabilities, gaining

such a distinction as this, though the thought naturally
arises that it is a pity Ireland cannot retain the services
of such clever young men. We want a University that
cculd turn the exceptional gifts of such as Mr. Gerald
Byrne into nation-making channels. However, if Ire-
land has little to offer brilliant young men, they will
go even as far afield as China. We congratulate Mr.
and Mrs. John Byrne, 11 Annesley Street, Belfast, on
their son’s brilliant performance.

We have received an interesting publication from
Messrs. Hely’s, Ltd., entitled ‘ Trish Rural Life and
Industry.”” The volume, which is published at 3s. 6d.
net, was issued in connection with the Home Industries
section of the International Exhibition. Many ques-
tions touching the life of Rural Ireland are dealt with
in its pages by writers with special knowledge of various
branches of Art and Industry. Mr. Wm. Cronin, of the
Dripsey Mills, tells how a small woollen factory could
succeed. Music as a means of brightening the social
aspect of country life is dealt with by Mr. W. J. Butler.
Colonel Everard writes on Tobacco Growing in Ireland.
Dr. Joyce on Home Industries in Ireland 1,000 years
ago. Dermod O’Brien on Irish Traditional Dancing.
Mr. R. M. Martin on the Manufacture of Lace and
Crotchet in Ireland, ete., etc.

The next Sunday Orchestral Concerts takes place on
the 17th at the Antient Concert Rooms ab 4 o’clock.
The vocalist will be Miss Alice Rafter, who will
sing songs by Mozart and Beethoven. Mr. Clyde
will contribute two ’cello solos. The

orchestra will play an overture by Cherubini, entitled
‘“Les Abencérages.””  ‘‘ Beethoven’s Symphony, No.
2,”” will be performed in its entirety. This symphony
was written about the time when Beethoven’s deafness
was beginning to become a very tragical matter to him;
but no despondency can be traced in the work, which
displays throughout a spirit of confidence and tran-
quillity of mind. The concert closes with Wagner’s
overture to the ‘“ Meistersingers.” .

The ‘‘ Enniscorthy Echo " is, we understand, more or
less a Martynette or ‘“ Hungarian ’’ organ. We express
ourselves thus guardedly, as we rather suspect that it
does not exactly know what it is itself. Anyway, Mr.
Donohoe, J.P., of Enniscorthy, is interested in it. We
notice a lot of auction advertisements in a recent issue.
There are a number of parcels of grazing land to be sold.
Amongst the advertisements we note one announcing an
auction of 60 acres of rich grass for eleven months,
another ‘‘ prime grazing land for eleven months.”” We
think we have heard that phrase, ‘‘eleven months,”
before. Isn’t there an agitation about it? Ilas it not
something to do with the campaign of Mr. Ginnell,
M.P., and some connection with the forthcoming pro-
secution of Mr. Farrell, M.P.? Is advertising sales of
grass lands for eleven months a plank in the ‘‘ Hun-
gavian ”’ policy, and if not, how is it that the valiant
“ Enniscorthy Echo ’’ advertises such sales? We wonder
what does Mr. John Sweetman, one of the Vice-presi-
dents of the ‘“ National Council,”” think of our Ennis-
corthy contemporary?

There was ‘“ A Grand Operatic and Ballad Concert ’
at Newry on the 4th inst. in aid of the local Christian
Brothers’ Schools. There is not even one Irish item on
the programme. A Mr. O’Brien was down to sing
““ London Lights,”” but he forgot his music, and sang
something else, and also a thing called ‘‘ Rooney’s
Spree.”” There is a long account of the “ exceptionally
brilliant affair ’’ in a local paper, ‘‘ The Frontier Sen-
tinel,”” or should it be the ‘‘ Sleeping Sentinel;’’ any-
way, it did not keep its Irish eyes open at this non-
TIrish concert. We are told that Rev. Brother Dempsey
brought ‘“ to our town some of the best musiczcl talent
Ireland can produce.” Tt was a wonder then that he
did not engage someone to sing at least one song in Irish.
Are all the Christian Brothers at Newry West Britons,
or must we place the responsibility of this programme
sclely on the shoulders of Rev. Brother Dempsey ?

Some months ago we visited the Munster Institute,
and wrote an article upon it in our series on ‘‘ The
Department in Being.”” = We are glad to see that four
Mimnster Institute pupils distinguished themselves in the
butter-making contests at the recent Dairy Show at
Islington, London. Four pupils from the Munster
Institute entered in the class open to those who had
not previously won a prize at the show, and all four
carried off prizes. The competition was arranged in two
sections. In one section the Munster Institute compe-

titors carried ‘off the first and second prizes; and in the .

other section they carried off the first and third. These
are distinctions of greater significance in many respects
than Intermediate gold medals, and these sort of things.
The names of the young ladies are—Miss M. Kava-
nagh, Miss E. McEntaggart (winners of the two first
prizes); Miss J. Cassidy (winner of second prize), and
Miss J. O’Hanlon (winner of third prize). These dis-
tinctions were won in a competition in which nearly 200
butter-makers from all parts of the three kingdoms, in-
cluding pupils of the leading British dairy schools, took
part.

We have received a copy of Mr. John J. Horgan's 
excellent book" Great Catholic. Laymen." The book bas 
now entered the second edition, which we have no 
doubt will receive as cordial a reception from the public 
as the. first. It is published, as before, by the Catholic 
Truth Society, Dublin. 
Our friends, the woodworkers of Kilkenny, are still

IRELAND AT WORK.

CLOTH-MAKING IN KILKENNY.

HE Kilkenny Woollen Mills were designed from a
model mill in Yorkshire, England, but with fur-
ther improvements. The building cost about £6,000,
the machinery about £7,000, and at the present
time there is in the course of being put up machinery to
the value of a further £2,000. The woollen mills are
all on the ground floor, and cover a square area.
Everything looks spick and span, and ‘‘ you could eat
your dinner off the floor.”” For the entire floor of the
factory is concrete.

There were other mills in Kilkenny at one time.
There was a flax mill, a tuck mill, corn mills, and
there was, and is, I believe, a woollen mill in a small and
spasmodic way at the other side of the city. Still I
suppose I am right, broadly speaking, in saying that
even the tradition of woollen-mill work had grown very
faint in Kilkenny, though the mothers of some of the
girls in the Kilkenny Woollen Mills worked in woollen
mills in their day. Indeed, one old woman came to the
mills on the day of the opening and said: “ For God’s
sake lot me look in; I want to see a good weaving
machine before I die.” :

The history of the establishment of the Kilkenny
Woollen Mills is so fresh in the public mind that I can

ass it over in a sentence or two. Captain Otway
Cuffe, the present Mayor of Kilkenny, was the prime
mover in it. Countess Desart took a substantial, as
well as a keen interest in it, as she afterwards did in
the Kilkenny Furniture Industry, and Very Rev. Canon
Doyle, P.P., of Kilkenny, besides being a shareholder
‘n the woollen mills, did more than one man’s part for

the establishment of the industry. The shares in the
Company are held by people in various parts, not only
of the country, but of the world—in America, London,
Dublin, and, of course, Kilkenny.

With the exception of worsted—and worsted goods
are only a small proportion of the total output—all the
cloth is made in the factory from the raw wool. In the
case of worsted the yarn is imported and the cloth is
only woven and finished.

It was on last Thursday that I paid my second visit
to Kilkenny. The manager of the mills, Mr. A. B.
Hunter, went in to meet me at the station, but he did
not know me, and I did not know what manner of man
he was, so when I drove out to the mills looking for
Mr. Hunter, I was told that he was in the city looking
for me! To make a long story short, we met later on,
as Mr. Kennedy, the Secretary of the Company, was
showing me over the place.

Descriptions of technical processes are very hard to
follow on paper; at least, I find them hard to follow.
But for those who like them, a brief outline of the
wool’s progress from the back of the gentle sheep to the
cloth that you see on show in the fashionable shops may
be interesting. The wool store at Kilkenny is a de-
tached and spacious building. ~Wool is bought from
the locality and neighbourhood, from Wexford, Tip-
perary, Carlow, Waterford, as well as from Kilkenny;
of course short wool from Australia and the British
Colonijes is imported to some extent, as foreign wool
is essential for some classes of goods. The wool is
washed ; it is dried and dyed. A good dye is an im-
portant matter, as everyone knows who has worn cloth
that has not kept its colour. Mr. Hunter, the manager,
showed me some strips of cloth parts of which were
covered and parts exposed to the air and light. Alzerine
dyes came out of the test in flying colours, and all the
wool and cloth at the Kilkenny mills is treated to
Alzerine dye; and in the matter of dye there is a dif-
ference on the cost of production of from 5d. to 2d. per
yard. Cheap and wasting dyes may look all right, but
the proof of the dye is in the lasting; and where does
sense come in in saving, say 3d. a yard, on cloth if it
loses colour in the wearing?

When the wool is dyed, it is blended, that is, wools
of different colours are collected and run through a big
machine familiarly called a ‘‘ shake willy *’; the suc-
cess of patterns and cloth largely depends on successful
blending. ~ The blended wool goes to the carding
machines, and having gone through I don’t know how
many rollers, it comes out as a sort of thin gauze, then
it becomes gauze in strips, and eventually develops into
untwisted thread on bobbins. The bobbins are removed
and put on the ‘“mule,”” and then the twisting com-
mences and the yarn is produced. I would not under-
take to attempt to describe a ‘‘ mule’’ in words to any-
one who has not seen one at work. There are four
““inules,”’ each containing 400 spindles—1,600 spindles
in all. The fixing of the design is, apparently, a
tedious process that I will not dilate upon for the very
good reason that, though Mr. Hunter did his best to
explain it to me, I am not in a position to pass an
examination in that branch. The design is fixed, any-
way, and then the weaving commences. At Kilkenny
they were, on the day of my visit, partly engaged in
weaving patterns; patterns are made in ranges, there
being 30, 40, as many as 60 different patterns to the
range. The patterns are about six inches by five, and
are submitted by the travellers to the wholesale houses.
The wholesale houses pick their fancy and the mills
execute the orders. The Kilkenny Woollen Mills make
thousands of samples. = After the weaving there are
various other processes—washing, milling.  There is
elaborate machinery for hot-air drying. The last process
of all for cloth that is not to be sent to the shrinker, is
the dekatir process for shrinking and adding a perma-
nent lustre.  However, this, and a lot more that
I could write of the processes that were explained to
me on the spot, would only be as Greek to those few

another are the prevailing patterns at the present time.

1907, intended for the winter season of 1908. Tweeds
of all kinds are the chief output of the factory. Saxonies
are also made, and serges and worsteds; and materials
for ladies’ costumes are, if not a speciality, quite a
considerable line in the Kilkenny factory’s output.

It is time to say something about the marketing of
the goods. Mr. Hunter was quite enthusiastic concern-
ing tho results to his firm of exhibicing at the recent
Aonac, organised by the London branch of the Gaelic
League. ‘“It opened the London trade to us,” said
Mr. Hunter, “ and it did a great deal of service.”” They
are exhibiting at the Royal Irish Industrial Association
Show at Manchester. Mry. Hunter had only just come
back from a business visit to London, and, as he had
opened up accounts with severa! whole_s:qe }101\ses in the
City and West End, he was quite optimistic of the pos-
sibilities of pushing Kilkenny goods over there. The
London Agent is Mr. J. J. Keane, 7 Broadway, Lud-
gate Hill, B.C. ;

The Kilkenny Woollen Mills have now two travellers
of their own on the road. One of them, Mr. O’Sulli-
van, late of Todd, Burns, Dublin, had only just been
appointed. The Company are certainly to be congratu-
lated on securing the services of such a good representa-
tive. Mr. O’Sullivan reminded me that we had met
previous to—I was going to say the dawn of
history—previous to the starting of the LEapEr. Tt was
in Killarney, of which place Mr. O’Sullivan is a native,
and T was either on my way to or my way back from
the Dingle peninsula; those were the days when I
carried a few pages of Seaona in one pocket and an
O’Growney in the other, and hurled Irish defiantly at
every preomin I encountered. The other representa-
tive is Mr. Cashin, formerly of Messrs. Clery’s, and he
has been with the Company since May last. Mr. O’Sul-
livan looks after Dublin, the North and the West, and
Mr. Cashin travels the South. Even though Rome
wasn’t built in a day, the Kilkenny Woollen Mills have
apparently made a lot of headway in the short period
of their existence. In Dublin, Todd, Burns, the Henry
Street  Warehouse, Clery’s, McBirney’s Pim’s,
Arnott’s, Ferrier, Pollock are amongst the houses that
have accounts with the mills. In Cork, Dwyers and
Co., Lyons and Co., Cash’s. In Waterford,
James Hearne and Co., Robertson, Ledlie
and Co. In Limerick, Cannock’s, and so on.
It is pleasant to hear that Kilkenny sup-
ports the mills well, for very often it is easier to sell a
hundred miles away than in the locality. Castlecomer
is an exceptionally good supporter.  Amongst other
places deserving of honourable mention from the Kil-
kenny Woollen Mills’ point of view, are Belfast, Tralee,
Ross, Enniscorthy ; the West is not bad, but the North,
with the exception of Belfast, has yet to be developed.
These may not be very interesting particulars, but they
give an idea of the reach already attained by this brand
new Kilkenny industry. The success of this ifdustry is of
special importance as it saw the light in the form of a
public Company, and the public Company was practi-
cally an outcome of the Irish-Treland movement.

This article has already run to such a length that I
had better sweep up the rest of my notes in a bundle.
The power is water, Turbine 137 H.P., but water is
fickle, so a new suction plant—120 horse-power—is being
set up. The immediate power is electricity, and there
are six several motors. The existence of six separate
sources of power does away with the necessity of a vast
system—an = expensive item, too—of shafting and
pinion wheels, and it has other advantages. * A new
stock warehouse is in the course of being built; for the
present stock-room is a part of the factory partitioned
off, and that space is wanted for the new looms.
In the stock-room we met quite a new addition
to ’ohg staff—Mr. McNamee from Navan, an enthusiastic
Gaelic Leaguer and brother to a well-known Gaelic
League worker in the capital of Bullock’s County.

ROGHEDA, alas, like many other Irish towns, is 
slowly decaying, its trade is now but a shadow 
of its former self. 
The hum of the Mills, the ring of 
the Foundries, the · clash of the machinery in the 
Breweri"es, al'e now like silent echoes of a busv past. 
But" the coming generation, reared in the school of 
Irish Ireland,_ may be able to rouse many of our Irish 
towns from their lethargy. The question is, how can 
'-"e once again make Drogheda a busy and prosperous 
town? First, we must find work for its sur1Jlus 1Jopu-
lation. 
We must establish .riew industries to give em-
ployment, and in order that these industries should 
succeed, they must be suited to the requirements of the 
locality. 
Such industries, I think, would be the sawing 
of timber, and the making of cabinets, chairs, and other 
wooden articles from this timber. 
The other industry 
I would suggest is tanning, and the making of boots and

THIS YEAR'S CROP RETURNS. 
The Statistics Branch of the Department of Agri-
culture give estimates of the probable yield from the 
different crops in the '' Quarterly Journal '' of the De-
partment published last month. It is regreUable to 
find that the Report describes the present harvest as 
not by any means up to the average. The past season 
taken all round was one oi the coldest, wettest, and 
most backward of any experienced for the past ·forty 
years. 
Crops were sown late, p·articularly in the wet 
land, and when they commenced to make progress in 
early summer, their growth was retarded by the un·-
naturally cold weather which prevailed. 
There was a 
spelf of sunshine in September, and under its influence 
harves~ operations proceeded satisfactorily. 
Except in 
the backward districts, the corn had been all safely cut 
and stacked by. the end of the month. 
Even under 
those satisfactory climatic conditions, however, the corn 
was found in anything but good condition. It had lain, 
or " lodged," to use the more familiar expression, and 
for that reason had become saturated with moisture. 
And, besides, the September weather, though sunshiny 
during the day, was in the mornings and evenings dull 
and foggy, and this · ungenial state of the· atmosphere 
told co~siderably on the stacked corn, as it was left to 
retain the moisture, through absence of a little wind or 
air currents. 
Then, when the continued downfall of 
rain followed in October, matters became infinitely 
worse; the crops in the late districts, ·only then being 
cut, were all but ruined; while in the earlier parts, 
where the. corn had been stacked, sprouting commenced 
and continued to the serious loss of quality in the grain. 
All told, the season of 1907 has been most disappoint-
ing to Irish farmers, and the agricultural ou~look is 
gloomy in ·the extreme. 
. 
Everything points to a hard winter, and dear prices 
for all classes of grain. 
The latter circumstance would 
be, of course, favourable to the fa1:mer if he had grain 
to sell in quantity, but with bad yield ,.:id w9rse :\lar-
vesting, his marketable produce is deficie.ui in quantity 
and of inferior quality--so that his profits will be very 
light notwithstanding prices are over the average. Of 
course too much reliance cannot be placed on the De-
partmental Returns in respect of the yield of crops. 
Even under the best conditions, as regards accurate 
replies to queries, such returns can only be regarded as 
an approximation. 
Unless by careful survey qf his land 
under crops, and accurate scale test as regards yield, a 
farmer can only give a vague guess as to p1.obable re-
turns. 
Then, when information respecting the latter is 
sought for publication by a Governmental Department, 
if obtained from a landlord source, it may be tinged 
with a roseate hue; but if a tenant farmer is apP.ealed 
to, perhaps he might be inclined to cut a little too low. 
Possibly the Department people take the opinion of 
both classes, and then strike a mea'n average: 
On the 
whole, the Returns may not, perhaps, be considered 
extravagant, but they certainly seem to be over the 
mark, as regards yield in some of the crops, and it would 
appear necessary to discount them a little if one is to 
arrive at a true estimate. 
As regards wheat, ihe reports are favourable. 
The 
yield of grain, in Ulster, -has been estimated at from 
6 to 10 barrels (20 stone) per statute a9re ; in Con-
naught, from 6 to 8 barrels; in Leinster, 7 to 12 bar-
rels, and in Munster 7 to 9 barrels. The average for 
the four provinces (says the R eport) would likely ap-
proximate as follows:-" Ulstei', 7 .12; Connaught, .7; 
Leinster, 9, and Munster 8 barrels." The above esti-
mates are high, even in a· good year ; but the probability 
is, that wheat was sown under advantageous circum-
stances early last winter, and the crop was not retarded, 
as other crops were, by the late spring. 
The wheat 
may, also, have ripened early and been harvested and 
threshed before the disastrous October rains. It must 
be further recollected that the crop is _grown to a 
limited extent, generally, through the country, and 
that it is only in the go-ahead tillage counties it is 
alloted fair acreage. 
·wheat is only sown in land 
liberally manured and well tilled the preceding season, 
so that it gets every chance, and aboYe every other crop 
it should give a fair · average return. 
Estimating this 
year's yield at 12 barrels the Irish acre, the crop would 
pay very well at present prices ; with a lesser yield, and 
lower prices, it could not be conside:·ed remunerative. 
The straw, when it can· be sold to advantage, makes a 
good return ; but, of course, unless a farm is placed con-
venient to a big town, from which manure is obtainable, 
it is bad practice to sell the straw off a farm. 
The 
Irish millers are now calling for home-grown wheat, 
and there is response made by farmers from certain 
parts of the country, who are inclined to give the crop 
the preference over barley, as the latter is disappointing· 
the present season, in respect of yield and prices. 
Why 
do not the small farmers, generally, who have land 
adapted to wheat, grow a little of it for hon1e use, and 
for calf. meal, as is being done by the smaller farmers in 
County Cork, according to the Report? 
The oat crop is reported to have done well on dry lea, 
but on manured land it was disappointing, while on 
cold, heavy, low-lying fields, the crop failed to stock 
propei·ly, and the spaces became occupied with weeds 
and grasses, which caused late maturity, and a bad 
yield both of grain and straw. 
A County Antrim cor-
respondent is quoted in the Report as follows:-" The 
worst crop on record," and he estimates-the yield at only 
5 to 6 cwt. per statute acre-which may be considered 
as equivalent to the failure of the crop altogether. 
There are good farmers in County Antrim, and the 
abave statement, embodied in a ~ovenunent Report, 
regarc;ling the failure of the oat crop in the County, 
sounds ominous as regards yield in the o!,her counties, 
and it is not to he wondered at. that the market value 
of the cereal has a steady upward tendency. . it is only-

more than all the I. G.’s who preceded him to raise the
character: and standard of the police To try to make
the police semi-military, ruins it as a civil force. Since
1900 the volice have been travelling soldierwards.
.They spend their time principally arranging and attend-
~ ing shootmg matches, aiming at bulls-eyes for many
2 hpurs_ at a time, organizing cycle tournaments and long
bicycle rides, sometimes thirty or forty in number away
from their stations, attending boxing clubs and such
. -edifying encounters, and calling at hotels and public
houses, which, of course, becomes quite necessary when
- men are away many hours from barracks, eating dinners
a_ud drinking the health of distinguished persons. This
line of life has a fascination for a young inexperienced
man who never asks himself whither does it tend? The
sergeant in charge may not see the utility of this sys-
tem, but then the I. G. permits it. The men must have
time to repair themselves and their machines. The
hilts of swords must have a peculiar brightness, and
the precise bend of the back at ‘attention’’ must be
acquired. Much time is given to learning the fads of
the officers as they move from place to place, and prac-
tising how to please them. This system does not suggest
high ideals or serious consideration to men’s minds
regarding the aims of existence, nor does it put money
in a man’s purse, so that, perhaps, the men find them-
selves in need of an increase of pay to recoup them for
the unprofitable investments made under the influence of
such exciting conditions.

There is widespread discontent in the police, and try-
ing to force it beneath the surface merely causes it to
burn more intensely. The remedy must be sweeping
if it is to be effective. It requires about fifteen or
twenty years’ experience to make a fairly capable police-
man, and many men are never fit. What about the
able County Inspectors and District Inspectors who have
given from twenty to thirty years’ service, and who
know the duties of the police? Have they no grievance

not utter a word of complaint or a pretext would be
discovered for running them out of the force and de-
priving the public of their experience. Are County
Inspectors Williamson of Wicklow, Foley of Kilkenny,

charge of the police? Was Mr. Watters, late A.I. G.,
incapable of commanding the police? Even an Irish
Protestant seems to inherit a disqualification for com-
mand in his own country, associating the people with
the administration of the Law and giving them confi-
dence in it indeed!

SAYING THE GRAIN.

IT is a long while since the harvest was so late as in
this year, and even this second week of November
finds the farmer with nearly half his grain lying in the
haggards, awaiting the thresher, while some of the less-
lucky men see their corn still in stouks upon the open
field, at the mercy of November wind and rain. It is
through the pale sunlight of our late autumn mornings
that the corn carts file in slow, long line from the farm
to the grain-merchants’ stores, in the neighbouring mar-
ket town. Between nine and ten o’clock any morning for
the last month you could not go for a walk near one of
the small towns without meeting those carts, loaded with
sacks of grain, drawn by all descriptions of quadrupeds,
the heavy, slow-paced plough-horse, the raw-boned nag,
the mare, stoop-shouldered, over-aged for any other
work, or the brisk jennet, or, finally, the steady-going
and imperturbable donkey.

Arrived at the store, they draw up under the top-
storey opening from which a crane slings out its hook to
draw their freights up. Here begins the unloading of
the harvest. The average cart-load is six bags of corn;
the bags are piled in order on a bedding of straw to
prevent shaking or slipping in transit, and at the cart’s
head there is a little hay for the horses to eat as they
patiently wait their turn to be relieved of their burdens.
The business of unloading is carried through deftly and
quickly, and enlivened by interchanges of wit, between
the man at the crane, who works by the day, and is,
therefore, in no hurry to get done, and the farmer who

_is anxious to get this load in and start back for another,
and yet another before the carly sunset.. At times
there occurs a hitch in the working of the pulley, or in
the fastening of a bag to the chain; and then, until all
is settled, the farmer fumes and frets, for he feels
never safe till his last bag is in the store and he can tell
himself that, for this year, his crops are saved. At
times, again, there are more serious acgldents. I re-
member once stopping to watch the perilous ascent of
one bag which seemed insecurely fastened. Tt swayed
upwards uncertainly to the midway qf its journey, fo_]-
lowed by the sharp, anxious eyes of its owner; in vain
he urged the pulley-man to “Hurry, quick, now, with
this one!”” The pulley-man was over-cautious in his
anxiety to avoid a jerk, so that half-way up the bag
suddenly slipped the hook and came thumping down to
the ground at its owner’s feet, bursting into a sea of
scattered golden drops upon the dusty roadway. This
was caused- by careless fastening, a carelessness which

but left a bit slack ; a few minutes after the first disaster
T saw a badly filled bag drawn jerkily up, dangie un-
certainly for a moment, then with a last jerk burst sud-
denly asunder, pouring a very shower of sun-bright
grain upon the farmer’s devoted head, while the empty
bag flapped ridiculously in the breeze like a flag at half-
mast.

The business of bringing in the grain is taken in
turns by the farmer himself or by his most trusted men,
who find it a pleasant change from the common round
of their day’s work. While waiting their turn they sit
or stand around the corn-store, exchanging views as to
the tervible bad luck they had this year, and fighting
over again their battles with that arch-enemy of the
agriculturalist—the weather. In one little town I know
of, blessed with a single grain-store, I have watched a
motley crowd gathered as to some solemn rite or festival
to ““ save the crop.”” The store stands in a by-way, and
the carts come down that passage to deliver their loads;
the grey wall of the high building is relieved by a great
growth of creeper, flashing crimson and gold in the
autumn sun, and throwing quite a blaze of mellow light.
over the little lane, and the dun sacks of corn, the
patient horses and the no less patient men. Seen in
that glow, the men’s faces assume a more intense ex-
pression, their characteristics strike one more forcibly,
and upon every mouth and brow there seems to be:
written a grim, patient hunger; the hunger of the
earth-bound, to whom the earth has been no generous
mother, the patience of the land-born for ever struggling
to subsist upon that land. They sit or stand, talking
more cheerfully than one might think, for the Irish-
man’s one excuse for existence arises from his inex-
haustible hope; of the rains, and the cold, and the sad
acres of corn that is lodged, or stouks that have rotted’
in the swift October rain; of the coming winter, and
the barren outlay of the year’s dead months, of debts
that must be paid, and ‘“soft’’ children that must be-
reared ; they speak of all with a patient hopefulness,
turning from such serious subjects to speak in lighter:
vein of the little merrymakings which come even with
the worst harvest, or of the latest and most burning of
economic questions, the problem of drink at threshings.

They cannot sit that way long, now; for the nights
close in sudden and swift ; the passage darkens, and the
red glow fades from the creeper on the wall before the
shadows that are beating up from the night. The pas-
sage is soon silent and empty, peopled only with echoes
and shades; and yet but little of this year’s grain has
been saved. IkAroS.

Qur correspondent further writes :—* Mullingar wants
a man—a very man—who shall make men of its males.
Irishmen, of course; they must be that or nothing. He
must be a real captain of industry, a man, say, who
would seize upon the deserted jail and the imaginations
of his fellow-townsmen at one and the same time. In
the one he might turn Mullingar heifers into potted
meat or bovril, and the other he would fire with a local
patriotism that would fructify in the form of a town
such as the Mullingar of my really fond imagining. This
would have a good town hall, a free library, a public
baths, a band stand, a gymnasium, factories and well-
built workmen’s houses. There would be a noble cathe-
dral in due time to express devotion and serve the needs
of a growing population. And everything would be lit
by electricity developed from the plentiful water power
that surrounds Mullingar. But there must come forth
the man to embody this idea, and to carry it on to its
censummation with all Mullingar for his willing helper.
He would not do it for them; he could not, or if he
could it would be almost as well that he did not. For
unless a people build their own city he labours in vain
who builds it for them. Well, that’s ‘a Castle in Spain,’
isn’tit? Easy enough-to devise and set forth in outline
far away from Mullingar. Yet it could be done, it could
be built in solid stone—even by the men of Mullingar.

CORRESPONDENCE.

SixN FE'N PRACTICE AND PREACHING.
London,
8th November, 1907.

DearR Sir,—In the propaganda of Sinn Fein great
importance is attached to the anti-recruiting crusade.
But, according to a current rumour, a committee mem-
ber of a branch recently formed in Clapham is a volun-
teer. If the rumour is true, this branch is guilty of the
grossest sham. No matter what brand of political
faith one professes, every honest-minded Irishman is
interested in the purity of Irish public life, and is,
therefore, entitled to know from the Secretary of the
branch whether its membership is open to volunteers,
notwithstanding its theoretical opposition to the British
Army.—Respectfully yours W. B. HENNESsY.
